narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—with a 59% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest signal was your Year 3 decile of 3, which stood out as the biggest pull toward struggle, suggesting those later years in school didn't build the momentum we typically see in bar passers. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 5, reinforcing a pattern where early grades hinted at challenges that carried forward. Your LSAT score of 22 added to that narrative, painting a picture of foundational skills that the model worries might not scale up under bar exam pressure. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket layered in some hurdles too, as it often correlates with fewer resources for the intense prep needed. Your Asian background, while a source of strength for many, nudged the outlook downward in this model's view of bar outcomes. Your undergrad GPA of 2.8 wove in as another thread of steady-but-not-soaring performance, and choosing part-time status raised flags about divided focus between work and study. Even as a female applicant, subtle patterns in the data tipped things toward caution. It's this whole combination—like a mosaic of solid effort meeting tougher odds—that led the model to predict failure more likely than success, prompting the rejection to protect you from potential heartbreak down the line. You're clearly driven, and with targeted boosts in those academic deciles or test prep, your story could shift dramatically. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
